critic_raw:
1. The split strategy uses stratified random sampling across all available years with no temporal ordering. For a 5-year cardiology cohort, this is a critical flaw: ECG technology, interpretation standards, and recording protocols evolve over 5 years, and random mixing means patients whose ECGs were recorded later in the cohort can appear in training. The 12-month cardiac event outcome window means that a patient recorded in year 4 with known year 4-5 outcomes can train the model, while a patient recorded in year 1 (whose outcome was observed before the rest of the cohort was collected) appears in the test set. This is temporal leakage.

2. The CNN receives raw ECG waveforms (12-lead signals), while the logistic regression baseline receives only clinical features with no ECG-derived features — not even standard clinical measurements like QT interval, ST elevation, or PR interval. The design explicitly states this is to "measure raw waveform analysis (CNN) against non-waveform clinical data (LR)." This comparison does not test the hypothesis that CNN-based waveform analysis outperforms traditional ECG analysis; it tests whether ECG data (in any form) adds value over non-ECG clinical data. A clinically meaningful comparator would give LR access to standard clinician-extracted ECG features, not deny it ECG information entirely.

3. The label (cardiac event within 12 months) is ascertained from the same hospital system as the ECG recordings. Patients who had their ECG at this hospital and subsequently had a cardiac event were hospitalized at the same or affiliated facility for that event. Patients who transferred care, moved, or had events at unaffiliated hospitals may be labeled as non-events. This creates a systematic outcome ascertainment bias: the model effectively predicts "cardiac event documented in this hospital system" rather than "cardiac event," which can be predicted partly by whether the patient remains a patient of this system — a feature correlated with socioeconomic stability, insurance type, and geography, all of which are available to both models.

4. The 5-second ECG truncation (1,250 samples at 250 Hz) discards the vast majority of a standard clinical ECG recording and excludes rhythms that require longer observation windows (e.g., paroxysmal AF, bradyarrhythmias that manifest intermittently). The choice of 5 seconds is not justified relative to the 12-month outcome window; rhythm abnormalities predictive of cardiac events over a year often require longer recordings or multiple recordings. Truncating to 5 seconds may systematically eliminate the very signal the CNN would need to exploit.

5. AUROC is the primary metric, but cardiac risk stratification for the stated use case (flagging the top 20% of patients for intervention) is better served by precision at a fixed recall or AUPRC, especially given that outcome prevalence is not stated. If the event rate is low (e.g., 5%), AUROC can be high even when the positive predictive value at the 20% threshold is poor. The clinical utility metric should be precision at the 80th percentile predicted risk, not AUROC.
all_issues_raised: Stratified random split without temporal ordering allows 5-year ECG cohort to mix future recording contexts into training data
Logistic regression baseline receives no ECG features, testing ECG value vs. no-ECG rather than CNN-waveform vs. clinician-extracted ECG features
Outcome ascertainment limited to same hospital system conflates 'cardiac event' with 'cardiac event documented in this system', introducing care-continuity bias
5-second ECG truncation eliminates rhythm information relevant to 12-month cardiac risk, possibly removing the signal the CNN most needs
AUROC primary metric is insensitive to the clinical use case (top-20% flagging), which requires precision at fixed recall or AUPRC

Comparison tests different feature sets (ECG waveforms vs. clinical features only), not model architectures; any AUROC difference is attributable to information content, not CNN vs logistic regression

Stratified random split on 5-year data may not enforce patient-level isolation; if the same patient appears in training and test across different years, model benefits from memorized cardiac baselines

12-month follow-up window creates survival bias; patients with ECGs in years 4-5 may have censored outcomes, and excluding them selects for patients with complete follow-up correlated with better prognosis
Fixed QRS detection and lead alignment algorithm may fail silently on arrhythmia or noise-corrupted recordings with no quality gate or detection described
AUROC on a cardiology-enriched cohort does not translate to general population or screening performance; outcome prevalence directly determines PPV, and precision-recall AUC should be co-primary
Comparison confounds input modality (ECG waveform vs. clinical features) with model architecture (CNN vs. LR) — the value of waveform analysis is not isolated
Stratified random split on a cardiology cohort may allow same patient to appear in train and test despite stated intent to prevent this
QRS detection and alignment may systematically degrade CNN performance on ischemic cases where QRS morphology is abnormal — preprocessing bias against the targeted signal
Validation cohort described as 'year 4' in Section 6 contradicts the random 15% sampling strategy in Section 2 — split strategy is ambiguous
